爱情，库尔特：《沃恩古特情书》，1941年至1945年，由伊迪丝·冯内古特（Edith Vonnegut）编辑；兰登书屋，240 pp。，$ 35
 大多数身穿制服的男人从未见过死亡。但是印第安纳波利斯的私人头等舱库尔特·冯内古特（Curt Vonnegut）于1944年12月在大爆炸战役中被捕，并作为奴隶劳工运往德累斯顿。几十年后，他将描述到达“大多数美国人见过的最美丽的城市。天际线错综复杂，妖and迷人，荒唐……在他的棚车后面有人说“ Oz。”他的战俘小组被安置在一个地下肉柜里，并被告知要记住它的街道地址：SchlacthofofFünf。屠宰场五。
 一个月后，欧洲的建筑珠宝盒不见了。 1945年2月，战争险胜，1 249架盟军飞机向该市投放了4000吨高炸药，摧毁了100,000座建筑物。平民伤亡是巨大的，而且仍在争论中：可能有25,000，也许有60,000。成为炮弹的受害者就像落入阳光下。有些人立即窒息而死。其他的则炸成骨头，所有的东西都缩小成黑褐色的小块。儿童大部分都被气化了。
 在党卫军的枪口下，冯内古特（Vonnegut）从废墟中挖出了德累斯顿的死人，这是康奈尔大学生物化学专业的熵与衰变的极端教程。他只有22岁，在一个以德语为母语的繁荣家庭中长大，以其与旧世界的联系而自豪。德累斯顿的丧葬之火成为了他道德观的核心和后来的文学名望的引擎，但是见证的代价几乎是无法承受的，而且每个爱他的人也付出了代价。
 两个女人使他保持理智。他的大姐姐爱丽丝是他的缪斯女神，是双胞胎。他未来的妻子简·考克斯（Jane Cox）自幼时起就是朋友，也是一名未来作家，成为他的文学裁判。他在1943年告诉她：“我们关系的一个独特特征是，您是这个世界上我喜欢写的那个人。如果我写过任何东西，无论好坏，都会被写成。你在想。 …我们有七个孩子xxxxxxx。” V-J日之后的两周，他们结婚并长大，是的，在爱丽丝死于癌症两天前，父亲的通勤火车落入纽瓦克湾后，他们孤儿养育了七个孩子，其中三个是自己的孩子，四个是侄子。
 冯内古特（1922-2007）在高​​中或大学就不再教书；诺顿美国文学选集长6,448页，仅包括他最著名的小说《五号屠场》的第一章。他的职业生涯有大量文献记载：传记，信件收藏，印第安纳大学的大量档案。然而，一个核心谜团仍然存在：库尔特，这位无瑕的常春藤兄弟男孩，如何成为银河系讽刺作家冯内古特。
 最近发现了两个缺失的环节。第一本是由冯内古特（Vonnegut）及其家人编纂的长达84页的信纸和本剪贴簿，长期藏匿，于2018年在佳士得拍卖行出售给私人买家。第二本是这本情书，由他的长女伊迪丝·冯内古特（Edith Vonnegut）回收，她在母亲阁楼上摸索着发霉的睡袋和旧目录时，发现“一个用脆黄色胶带密封的白色礼品盒”。 
里面有226种战时新闻，其中有些是打字的，一些是手写的和插图的。她告诉我们：“我找到了爸爸的露西，” “他即将成为谁的最早线索。”在这份精美的汇编中，每个字母都被完整地拍摄，这是退缩战争中铅笔涂鸦的理想选择。
 他花了24年的时间在小说中表达自己在地狱中所看到的一切，但简的早期建议清楚地表明，学徒小说家冯内古特（Vonnegut）是合拍作品。 （J恩在许多交往中一败涂地，可惜，她在战时OSS方面的工作很少描述。）他沉迷，犹豫，盘旋。她比较有野心，扮演治疗师和中士，意识到自己的巨大需求最能满足轻快的命令：读托尔斯泰，读陀思妥耶夫斯基，继续自己的命运。
 “你吓到我了，”他曾经对她说，“当你说我将要不断创造1945年的文学作品时。天使，如果它往后走，你会坚持吗？”
 爱情，库尔特（Kurt）是对冯内古特（Vonnegut）学习的绝佳补充。它也是文学少年。但正如冯内古特（Vonnegut）所知，第二次世界大战是一场儿童战争，由很小的孩子打过仗，没有战斗员公平竞争：“我们真正的婴儿是：十七岁，十八岁，十九岁，二十岁，二十一岁。我们面无表情，作为战俘，我认为我不必经常刮胡子。”
 印第安纳州的梦想家们没有圆满的结局。数年的贫困以令人头疼的名声封顶，注定了他们的婚姻。他们俩都重新结婚了，简开心了，库尔特没有结婚。随着朝鲜流血进入越南，他的忧郁情绪和反战信念都加深了，最终导致了《五号屠场》（Slaughterhouse-Five，1969）的流派混搭：士兵的故事，时间旅行的嬉戏，目击者的文学作品，德累斯顿的死人，每一次恐怖和荒诞都助长了冯内古特坚决镇压的宇宙愤怒，只有真正的中西部人才能做到。
 他在1981年说：“一种或另一种方式，每一个被杀的人我得到两到三美元。我从事一些业务。”
 1941年的库尔特会感到震惊。 1945年的库尔特（Kurt）急切地想起正常人的感觉，可能只会点头。在这两个日期之间的旅程还映射了分隔的距离，例如，私人诺曼·梅勒（Norman Mailer），文员兼打字员和想家的奴隶劳工在德累斯顿的避难所里挖掘身体部位。对于文学而言，冯内古特拥有最需要的生命线是多么幸运。爱，库尔特，是的，还有爱，简。
note_en:
Love, Kurt:  The Vonnegut Love Letters, 1941–1945 edited by Edith Vonnegut; Random House, 240 pp., $35
 Most men in uniform never see death. But Private First Class Kurt Vonnegut of Indianapolis was captured at the Battle of the Bulge in December 1944 and shipped to Dresden as slave labor. Decades later, he would describe arriving at “the loveliest city that most of the Americans had ever seen. The skyline was intricate and voluptuous and enchanted and absurd … Somebody behind him in the boxcar said, ‘Oz.’ ” His POW group was housed in an underground meat locker and told to memorize its street address: Schlacthof Fünf. Slaughterhouse Five.
 One month later, Europe’s architectural jewel box was gone. In February 1945, the war nearly won, 1,249 Allied planes dropped 4,000 tons of high explosives on the city, destroying 100,000 buildings. Civilian casualties were immense, and are still debated: maybe 25,000, maybe 60,000. To be a victim of firebombing is like being dropped into the sun. Some people instantly suffocate. Others fry, bones and all, shrinking into small, dark brown lumps. Children are vaporized, mostly.
 At SS gunpoint, Vonnegut dug Dresden’s dead from the rubble, an extreme tutorial in entropy and decay for the Cornell biochemistry major. He was only 22, raised in a prosperous German-speaking family proud of its Old World ties. The funeral pyres of Dresden became the core of his moral vision and the engine of his later literary fame, but the price of witnessing was nearly unbearable, and everyone who tried to love him paid it, too.
 Two women kept him sane. His big sister Alice was his muse, a spiritual twin. His future wife, Jane Cox, a friend since kindergarten days, also a would-be writer, became his literary umpire. “One peculiar feature of our relationship is that you are the one person in this world to whom I like to write,” he told her in 1943. “If ever I do write anything of length—good or bad—it will be written with you in mind. … And let’s have seven children xxxxxxx.” Two weeks after V-J Day, they married and raised, yes, seven children, three of their own plus four young nephews, orphaned after their father’s commuter train fell into Newark Bay two days before Alice died of cancer.
 Vonnegut (1922–2007) isn’t taught much anymore, in high school or college; the  Norton Anthology of American Literature, 6,448 pages long, includes only the first chapter of his best-known novel,  Slaughterhouse-Five. His career is heavily documented: biographies, collections of correspondence, a vast archive at Indiana University. Yet a core mystery endures: how Kurt, the feckless Ivy frat boy, became Vonnegut, satirist to the galaxy.
 Two missing links have recently come to light. The first is an 84-page scrapbook of letters and ephemera compiled by Vonnegut and his family, long hidden, sold to a private buyer at Christie’s in 2018. The second is this collection of love letters, recovered by his eldest daughter, Edith Vonnegut, who, while exploring a drift of moldy sleeping bags and old catalogs in her mother’s attic, found “a squashed white gift box sealed with brittle yellowed tape.”
 Within were 226 wartime missives, some typed, some handwritten and illustrated. “I found the Lucy of Dad,” she tells us. “The earliest inklings of who he was going to become.” In this handsome compilation, each letter is photographed in full, a perfect choice for penciled scribbles from a fading war.
 He took 24 years to express in fiction what he saw in hell, but Jane’s early advice makes clear that Vonnegut, apprentice fictioneer, was a co-production. (Jane’s side of many exchanges has been lost, alas, and her wartime OSS work is little described.) He broods, hesitates, circles. She, the more ambitious, plays therapist and drill sergeant, aware that his colossal neediness responds best to brisk command: read Tolstoy, read Dostoyevsky, get on with your destiny.
 “You scare me,” he told her once, “when you say that I am going to create the literature of 1945 onwards and upwards. Angel, will you stick by me if it goes backwards and downwards?”
 Love, Kurt is a terrific addition to Vonnegut studies; it is also literary juvenilia. But as Vonnegut knew, World War II  was a children’s war, fought by the very young, with noncombatants fair game: “What infants we really were: seventeen, eighteen, nineteen, twenty, twenty-one. We were baby-faced, and as a prisoner of war I don’t think I had to shave very often.”
 The Indiana dreamers got no happy ending. Years of poverty capped by head-spinning fame doomed their marriage. Both re-wed, Jane happily, Kurt not. As Korea bled into Vietnam, his gloom and his antiwar convictions both deepened, culminating in the extravagant genre mash-up of  Slaughterhouse-Five (1969): the soldier’s tale, the time-travel romp, the literature of witness, the reparative writing for the Dresden dead, each horror and absurdity fueling a cosmic rage that Vonnegut rigidly suppresses, as only a true Midwesterner can.
 “One way or another,” he said in 1981, “I got two or three dollars for every person killed. Some business I’m in.”
 The Kurt of 1941 would be shocked; the Kurt of 1945, desperate to remember what normal felt like, might only nod. The journey between those dates also maps the gap separating, say, a Private Norman Mailer, clerk-typist, and the homesick slave laborer mining for body parts in the Dresden charnel house. How lucky for literature that Vonnegut had a lifeline when most needed.  Love, Kurt, yes, but also  Love, Jane.
title: 讽刺银河系

note:
嘿!今天，我为您准备了一些不同的东西。这是关于领英（LinkedIn）怪异世界的部分内容-从名人打工者到感觉不合适的奇怪社交功能。这是Doist高级市场经理Fadeke Adegbuyi的客座文章，他是互联网文化的忠实拥护者。我认为您会喜欢它！ 😆
         尽管我们对该平台感到困惑，但人力资源部门和招聘人员的热情拥护仍然确保了该平台的成功。尽管由于大流行而被迫解雇1000名员工，但他们的第四季度收入仍然增加了10％。截至2020年第三季度，该公司拥有超过7.22亿用户，真正做到了国际化-仅26％的成员位于北美。 LinkedIn的收入主要由招聘解决方案，带薪工作清单和广告驱动，因此知道如何吸引企业。 LinkedIn不需要您喜欢它。
 但是，该公司的消费者方仍然处于长期的身份危机中。功能肿和奇怪的特质使该网站成为一个极为陌生的社交网络。我们许多人都有个人资料，但一次都没有登录过几个月。我们的“关于”部分指出了更好的方法。其他人似乎还活着，但状况良好，声称自己已经死亡。
  LinkedIn称自己为在线目的地，就像在五星级酒店举行的会议上那样，大型思想家发表大型演讲，在私人角落进行交易，您可以“建立并与专业网络互动”。但是我们很多人认为它不是一个有趣的小房子，镜子古怪，跳出惊喜和奇特的景点。我可以对您感兴趣吗？
  领英最近发布了Stories，这是从Instagram复制的垂直视频功能，可以更好地从Snapchat窃取。 LinkedIn建议用户“分享他们日常职业生涯的图像和短片。”在大规模迁移到偏远地区的工作中，由于全球大流行而发布的，目前尚不清楚我们是应该在厨房桌子上展示狭窄的工作环境，还是在第143分钟的打扰中，动静的幼儿园工作人员打扰到Zoom。
 这就引出了一个问题...谁在他们的手机上使用LinkedIn？该平台希望您相信名人。 LinkedIn上的一个永久性故事展示了一群工作爱好者– Mark Cuban，Arianna Huffington和Deepak Chopra –发布了他们工作时间的专业高清录像。
  标语结束了剪辑画面：“分享您的故事”。乍一看，我的1000多个连接中只有一个正在使用该功能。 
相比之下，Twitter在Fleets方面取得了早期成功，Fleets是他们自己的复制猫故事功能，用户已开始接受，并在子推文和狗屎贴子之间张贴婴儿照片和狗照片。模仿功能并不是一个坏策略。但是LinkedIn Stories在到达时就死了。董事会无人问这个关键问题：谁在乎？
  Clubhouse，Chalk和Rodeo等音频应用程序都指出了基于声音的社交功能。 Twitter于今年春季初发布了语音推文，目前正在开发音频空间功能。 LinkedIn已通过少数音频功能将自己的声音应用到语音中。就像平台上的大多数事物一样，它们感觉不合时宜。
 在个人档案中使用姓名发音功能背后的观点是正面的，但尚未达到预期目的的广泛采用。自该功能推出以来，我听说过名字最明显的人都在使用该功能，从插入Office的10秒剪辑到以2倍的速度播放歌曲片段。现在常见但意外的用例是令人愉快的巨魔：
 有一次，Jeff Hui使用该功能讽刺招聘人员的电梯音调
  本·泰勒（Ben Taylor）在他的名字中加入“ click 4 sound”（点击4声音）
  在人们宣布常春藤联盟协会，最高荣誉和在投资银行实习的网站上，对工作文化的厚颜无耻的无礼扭曲了LinkedIn所期望的崇高敬意。在LinkedIn上拖曳LinkedIn不同于BusinessTM的精美头像和公司精心设计的“体验”部分。具有讽刺意味的是，您可以在这个平台上表现专业水平。
 有时，LinkedIn似乎正在为拖钓的预期目的而积极地构建功能。在LinkedIn消息中，用户可以按住麦克风图标来记录他们的声音。音频片段是与陌生人开始专业对话的一种奇怪的私密选项。也许这就是重点。但是，试图通过过于熟悉的功能来加速关系是网络世界中不受欢迎的“个人接触”，而网络已经充满了假假。 
作为“网络不完全正确”的案例，“ LinkedIn的吉米”发送的无辜但荒唐的39秒音频消息迅速传播开来：
 “你好，坎迪斯，你好，早上好。你近来怎样？嗨Candice，这是我，LinkedIn的Jimmy。我之所以给您打这个快速语音消息，是为了谢谢您。非常感谢您在LinkedIn平台上接受了我。我们要做的就是互相学习。感谢您的好意。来自LinkedIn的Jimmy。”
  尽管它没有恶意，但在您寻找新工作机会以及前同事和同学的专业更新的同一个空间中遇到这种怪异的感觉就在眼前。这些东西会让你停下来说，我在哪里？
  从一开始，将专业人员聚集在一起的平台前提就是一个挑战。人们讨厌网络。而且，他们对此很不好。
  对于用户而言，LinkedIn的“联网”通常意味着收到大量InMail和连接请求。您可能会认识到其中一些或全部：
 糟糕的销售推销：销售人员和业务开发人员将滑入DM来兜售他们公司的产品，无论您是否合适。通常不会。
 专业人士：招聘人员会问高级员工是否对入门级职位感兴趣，或者对实习更感兴趣。 
万一发生这种情况：业内同行会建议进行连接“互惠互利”，但请不要先说明这些好处。
 濒临灭绝的商学院：招生团队现在可以在线上削减他们日益减少的MBA课程中令人垂涎的席位。根据您的个人资料和经验，他们认为您非常适合，并希望您申请。
 急切的求职者：有些人将LinkedIn消息视为替代的简历和求职信提交门户。这就是为什么您的消息中无休止的人员询问您部门之外或公司外部的角色的原因。
 LinkedIn团队：如果他们的电子邮件不足以通知您有关连接请求的信息，LinkedIn还会保留直接到您的InMail的电话。预计会出现尝试使用高级版的提示，用于宣传您公司的积分以及有关他们的其他想法的消息。
 命题：Linkedin在每个网站成为约会网站的悠久历史中立于不败之地。或者，是一个性骚扰人们的地方。如果您没有在InMail中收到这些邮件之一，则说明知道有人。
  过多的InMail和随机连接请求感觉就像是永无休止的琐事。您会收到足够的工作电子邮件，不再需要。 LinkedIn不断的交流只是您要做的实际工作之外的更多工作。
 Linkedin的收件箱无限不仅烦人，还降低了平台的可用性。除非您采用新的处理和回答问题的方式，否则如果真正的联系出现了，您将如何知道？ 
在LinkedIn时间轴上的任何给定时间，您都会看到一个由您认识的人分享的公司博客文章，来自您不认识的人的市场营销热度以及有关COVID-19的新闻故事。他们的时间表与其他社交网络类似，在这些社交网络中，您可以看到由您的人脉关系，关注的人和品牌制作或喜欢的帖子，以及宣传的帖子，而这些帖子是人们为获得您的支持而出现的。这种内容混乱的现象是奇异的，但不是非典型的-社会供稿可以在任何地方优化参与度。
 LinkedIn的独特之处在于它不是独特的。 “专业网络”仍然只是一个社交网络。尽管时间轴上的帖子主题可能涉及工作世界，但深入评论之后，您会看到实际的两极分化。在大流行期间，父母在在家工作时给予父母宽容的鼓舞人心的言论激怒了人们，表明孩子是个人的选择，不应将孩子的负担加在同事身上。在有关工作场所骚扰的文章下面，进行了富有同情心的讨论，讨论了为什么有人在浪漫拒绝后会自然地进行报复。一篇有关2020年美国总统大选的政治文章引述了前总统巴拉克·奥巴马（Barack Obama）是否真的是黑人。
  在集体讨论被人为取消的话题所困扰的时代，人们很容易在LinkedIn上发布使他们可燃或不可租借的东西。
  尽管这些评论对于在线文化而言并不是什么新鲜事物，但它们在专业网站上让人感到无所适从，人们可以使用其全名并准确披露他们的工作地点和所居住的城市。在LinkedIn的替代世界中，或者取消文化不存在，它的居民非常高兴地不知道，或者他们根本不在乎。
   Linkedin最奇怪的功能根本不是功能。这是一组特定的高级用户。
 每个平台都有其使用费。在Instagram上，它是影响者，美食家和摄影师。 Twitter属于创始人，新闻工作者，名人和喜剧演员。在LinkedIn上，它是招聘经理，招聘人员和企业主，他们在该平台上拥有强大的力量并广受人们的关注。人力资源经理是摇滚明星的平台的败坏说明了一切。
 在工作现场，他们是职位的提供者，永远不会错过用他们的专业事迹来重新吸引听众的机会：雇用没有经验的青少年，给压力重重的单身母亲一个为自己的家庭提供生活的机会，或者是过去候选人的不完美之处，使他们获得了千载难逢的机会。这些故事在如今公认的LinkedIn式讲故事中得到了极大的传承，每次讲义间隔一段，由工作人员带着救世主讲述。 
在LinkedIn上，工作不是个人与公司之间的交易，也不是填补9到5之间的空间的方式。在LinkedIn上，工作是肯定生活或挽救生命的机会，将人们从无意义的劳动或迫在眉睫的废墟。
  在这个故事中，直接来自LinkedIn的怪异世界，人们在2020年使用Skype，招聘经理在面试结束时主动提供绩效反馈，合同蓬勃发展并立即签订。读者可以用不到250个单词从一个故事中得到他们想要的一切：冲突，悲惨的自白，心转变，幸福的结局以及关于如何成为人性化的宝贵课程。
 在无限娱乐和电视热潮的年代，超过783,438人被转移到对一个可能不是真实的故事的反应上。
 然而，下面的评论却显示出深切的诚意，神圣的祝福和钦佩。模仿是一种奉承，这个故事是如此引人注目，以至于数十个人偷走并选择了它，以期获得自己的病毒时刻。 LinkedIn并没有将自己定位为“快速叮咬”的地方，但是，提要每天都充斥着此类故事，并且吸引了人们的关注-无论是在平台上还是在平台上。
 这些故事脱离了LinkedIn电影世界的背景，并在Twitter上分享，这些故事是嘲讽而不是钦佩之所在，并激发了模仿而不是模仿，这突显了LinkedIn与其他社交平台之间的差异。
   随着工作的发展，由Rockstar Recruiters和其他工作提供者推动的LinkedIn故事发展了。几年前浏览LinkedIn时显示的是逐行讲故事的内容，但轶事在主题上有所不同。然后，讲故事的钟摆转向了喧嚣的文化。时间表上充斥着团队建设的企业家，他们表现出最好的加里·维纳丘克（Gary Vaynerchuk）印象，劝告他们的追随者们更多地工作，加倍努力，并努力争取前进。
 招聘人员是看门人，他们使用LinkedIn来分享失去访问权限的最佳方法，并将您的简历扔到数字垃圾箱中。尽管该品牌仍然存在于平台上，但与微型叙事的新时代相形见—-移情正在发展。现在，该平台的病毒式传播故事是关于“让整个自我工作”，自我照顾以及在忙碌的世界中找到工作与生活的平衡。鉴于大流行导致工作方式发生了变化，微型讲故事的人告诉我们要对在幕后的同事产生同情心，并告诉我们远程工作倦怠的危险。 
考虑到随之而来的经济下滑以及求职压力的增加，招聘人员正以受挫的朋友的身份出售人力资源，在评估候选人并给他们梦the以求的求职信时，他们希望过去的工作经验少一些，毛茸茸。实际上，他们甚至更喜欢它！在领英之地，年轻，饥饿和愚蠢的人总是比打磨，专业和胜任的人更好的选择。 LinkedIn充满了关于工作的这些故事-真实，夸张和想象。
  简化为最简单的形式，LinkedIn是一种数字简历。个人资料包括您过去的工作经验，学历，技能和参考资料。帖子，评论和消息就像求职信。但是，我们长期以来一直认为，比您所工作的地点，去过的学校以及匆忙起草的工作清单，还有更好的方法来展示自己的能力。简历是过去时代的旧卷轴。如果LinkedIn是一个旨在证明您是专家的网站，那么它将与您可以做得更好的所有地方竞争。
 开发人员拥有GitHub，设计师使用Dribbble，而学者则维护其ResearchGate或Google Scholar个人资料。在Twitter上，首先是娱乐性的精神，然后是专业精神，这使得“联网”更加自然，甚至更加有效。另外，还有个人网站和投资组合。每个人都在建立个人品牌，他们只是没有在LinkedIn上这么做。
 专业平台无法正常工作，因为它包含了一系列矛盾。这是一个拥有数百万用户的网站，您认识的人都不会使用。在这里放置简历，但从不申请工作。
 但是，我们中的许多人都有个人资料，甚至可能会保留它，在我们进行专业工作时会增加新的角色。我们可能会冒险进入InMail Infinity，而不知道是谁邀请接受，最后还是全部接受。我们中有些人甚至“赞”更新的朋友和前同事的更新，他们也表现出专业精神，可能与我们对平台的矛盾一样。保持我们的页面最新，浏览消息，并尽最大努力确保我们的网络不会忘记我们，这是一种“以防万一”的心态，这种心态由某种程度的专业焦虑所驱动。
 如果有工作，您可能会失业。如果没有，您可能会找到一个。
  LinkedIn之所以怪异，是因为它试图使这种人质情况变得有趣。即使不是。当您添加故事，音频消息，DM，社交摘要或其他任何内容时，情况并非如此。如果真相得到承认，那么该平台可能不是替代性的宇宙，而是脚踏实地的：表演专业精神，求职和人际网络是工作的延伸而不是游戏。只要LinkedIn假装相反，我们还可以假装我们绝不会绝望地认真使用它。 
Fadeke Adegbuyi是Doist的高级市场经理，也是互联网文化的忠实拥护者。
note_en:
Hey! Today I have something different for you. It’s a piece about the weird world of LinkedIn — from celebrity workfluencers  to the strange social features that feel out of place. This is a guest essay from  Fadeke Adegbuyi, senior marketing manager at Doist and a fan of all things internet culture. I think you’re going to like it! 😆
         Despite our collective confusion about the platform, its enthusiastic embrace by HR departments and recruiters has ensured its success. Even though it was  forced to lay off 1,000 employees because of the pandemic, they still increased their Q 4 revenue by 10%. As of Q3 of 2020, the company has over 722 million users and is truly international––just 26% of their members are based in North America. With revenue largely powered by recruiting solutions, paid job listings, and advertising, LinkedIn knows how to appeal to businesses at scale. LinkedIn doesn’t need  you to like it.
 But, the company’s consumer-side remains in the midst of a long-standing identity crisis. Feature bloat and odd idiosyncrasies make the site a superbly strange social network. Many of us have profiles but don’t log in for months at a time. Our “About” sections point to better ways to be reached. Others, seemingly alive and well, have claimed on their pages to have died.
  LinkedIn bills itself as an online destination, like a conference held at a 5-star hotel where big thinkers give big speeches, deals get made in private corners, and you can “build and engage with your professional network.” But many of us see it as a not-so-funhouse, with wacky mirrors, jumpout surprises, and peculiar attractions. Can I interest you in a tour?
  LinkedIn recently released Stories, a vertical video feature copied from Instagram, who better stole it from Snapchat. LinkedIn  suggests users “share images and short videos of their everyday professional moments.” Released in the midst of a global pandemic during a mass migration to remote work, it’s unclear whether we should showcase cramped work setups on kitchen tables or restless kindergarteners interrupting the 143rd minute of a Zoom.
 Available only on LinkedIn's iOS and Android apps, this begs the question...  Who is using LinkedIn on their phone? The platform would like you to believe that celebrities are. A permanent Story from LinkedIn shows a reel of workfluencers ––Mark Cuban, Arianna Huffington, Deepak Chopra –– posting professionally shot high-definition videos of their work days.
  A tagline ends the montage: “Share your story.” At last glance, only a single one of my 1000+ connections is using the feature.
 In contrast, Twitter is seeing early success with Fleets, their own copy-cat story feature that users have been receptive to, posting baby photos and dog pictures in between subtweets and shitposts. Feature imitation isn’t a bad strategy. But LinkedIn Stories was dead on arrival. No one in the boardroom asked the critical question:  Who cares?
  Audio apps, like Clubhouse, Chalk, and Rodeo point to the power of sound-based social. Twitter released voice tweets earlier this spring and is currently working on an  audio spaces feature. LinkedIn has taken their own steps into voice with a handful of audio features. Like most things on the platform, they feel out of place.
 The sentiment behind their name pronunciation feature on personal profiles is a positive one, but hasn’t seen wide adoption for its intended purpose. Since the feature was launched, I’ve heard people with the most pronounceable of names use the feature for anything from inserting a 10-second clip from The Office to playing a song snippet at 2x speed. A now common but unintended use case is delightful trolling:
 At one point, Jeff Hui used the feature to ironically  shout an elevator pitch at recruiters
  Ben Taylor, who includes “click 4 sound” in his name,  rickrolls his profile visitors
  On a site where people declare Ivy League associations, summa cum laude status, and internships at investment banks, cheeky irreverence for work culture warps the throughline of respectability that LinkedIn intends. Trolling LinkedIn, while on LinkedIn, is a break from the BusinessTM of polished headshots and corporately crafted “Experience” sections. It’s ironic participation on a platform where you’re  meant to be performing professionalism.
 At times, it feels like LinkedIn is actively building features for the intended purpose of trolling. Within LinkedIn messages, users can hold down a microphone icon to record their voice. An audio snippet is an oddly intimate option for starting a professional conversation with a stranger. Perhaps that’s the point. But attempting to speed up relationships with overly familiar features is a “personal touch” unwelcome in the world of networking, already so fraught with faux pas.
 An innocent but outlandish 39-second audio message, sent by “ Jimmy from LinkedIn” went viral as a case of networking-gone-not-quite-right:
 “Hello Candice, how are you doing and good morning. How have you been? Hey Candice, this is me, Jimmy from LinkedIn. And the reason I was giving you this quick voice message call was to say thank you so kindly. I really appreciate that you have accepted me here on the LinkedIn platform. What we will do is learn from each-other. I appreciate your kindness. Jimmy from LinkedIn.”
  Though it doesn’t feel malicious, it’s just  off to encounter this kind of weirdness in the same space you seek new job opportunities and professional updates from former colleagues and classmates. It’s the stuff that makes you pause and say,  Where am I?
  The premise of the platform, bringing professionals together, is a challenging one from the start. People hate networking. Also, they’re bad at it.
  For users, LinkedIn “networking” often means receiving a barrage of InMail and connection requests. You might recognize some or all of these:
 The bad sales pitch: Sales people and business development people will slide into DMs to peddle their company’s products, whether you’re the right person to buy or not. Usually not.
 The professional neg: Recruiters ask senior level employees if they’re interested in entry level roles or, better yet, internships.
 The just-in-case connection: Industry peers will suggest it’s “mutually beneficial” to connect, but don’t specify these benefits up front.
 The endangered business school: Admissions teams are now online shilling once-coveted seats in their dwindling MBA programs. Based on your profile and experience, they think you’re a “great fit” and want you to apply.
 The eager seeker: Some people see LinkedIn messages as an alternative resume and cover letter submission portal. This is why that endless string of people in your messages inquire about roles outside your department… or outside your company.
 The LinkedIn team: If their emails notifying you about connection requests wasn’t enough, LinkedIn also maintains a direct line to your InMail. Expect prompts to try Premium, credits to advertise your company, and messages about whatever else they’re feeling.
 The proposition: Linkedin stands in the long-tradition of  every site becoming a dating site. Or, er, a place to sexually harass people. If you haven’t gotten one of these messages in your InMail, you know someone who has.
  Overfull InMail and random connection requests feels like a never-ending chore. You get enough work emails and don’t need any more. LinkedIn’s constant drip of communication is just more work on top of the real work you have to do.
 Linkedin’s inbox infinity isn’t just annoying—it detracts from the usability of the platform. Unless you’re embracing a new position of processing and answering everything that comes through, if a real connection came along, how would you know?
  At any given point on your LinkedIn timeline, you’ll see a corporate blog post shared by someone you know, a marketing sales pitch from someone you don’t, and a news story about COVID-19. Their timeline is similar in this way to other social networks, where you see posts made or liked by your connections, people and brands you follow, and promoted posts that people pay to get in front of you. This cacophony of content is bizarre, but not atypical –– social feeds optimize for engagement, here as everywhere.
 What’s uniquely odd about LinkedIn is that it is not unique. “The professional network” is still just a social network. Though the topics of the posts in your timeline may be on the world of work, diving into the comments, you can see polarization in action. Inspirational posts on giving parents grace while working from home during the pandemic have angry rants suggesting that kids are a personal choice and their burden shouldn’t be foisted upon colleagues. Beneath articles on workplace harassment, sympathetic discussions ensue on why someone might naturally retaliate after being romantically rebuffed. A political article about the 2020 US presidential election draws comments on whether former President Barack Obama was really Black.
  In a time when the collective conversation is consumed by the topic of human cancellation, people readily post things on LinkedIn that make them fireable or unhireable.
  While these comments are nothing new in terms of online culture, they feel out of bounds on a professional website where people use their full names and disclose precisely where they work and the city they live in. In the alternate universe of LinkedIn, cancel culture either doesn’t exist, its inhabitants are blissfully unaware, or they simply don’t care.
   The strangest of Linkedin’s features isn’t a feature at all; it’s a specific set of power users.
 Every platform has its royalty. On Instagram it's influencers, foodies, and photographers. Twitter belongs to the founders, journalists, celebrities, and comedians. On LinkedIn, it’s hiring managers, recruiters, and business owners who hold power on the platform and have the ear of the people. The depravity of a platform where HR Managers are the rockstars speaks for itself.
 On a job site, they’re the provisioners of positions and never miss the chance to regale their audience with their professional deeds: hiring a teenager with no experience, giving a stressed single mother a chance to provide for her family, or seeing past a candidate’s imperfections to give them a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. These stories are relayed dramatically in what’s now recognizable as LinkedIn-style storytelling, one spaced sentence at a time, told by job-givers with a savior complex.
 On LinkedIn, jobs are not a trade between an individual and a corporation, or a way to fill the space between 9 to 5. On LinkedIn, jobs are life-affirming or life- saving opportunities, rescuing people from a life of meaningless toil or imminent ruin.
  In this  story, straight from the weird world of LinkedIn, people use Skype in 2020, hiring managers give unsolicited feedback on performance at the end of an interview, and contracts are flourished and signed immediately. Readers get everything they would ever want out of a story in under 250 words: conflict, a tragic confessional, a change of heart, a happy ending, and a valuable lesson on how to #behuman.
 In the age of infinite entertainment and peak television, over 783,438 people were moved to leave a reaction on a story that’s probably not true.
 And yet, the comments underneath reveal a depth of sincerity, divine blessings, and extended admiration. Imitation is flattery and this story was so compelling that  dozens of people have stolen and co-opted it, hoping for their own viral moment. LinkedIn doesn’t market itself as a place for “quick bites,” but feeds are overflowing with these types of stories each day, and they capture attention—both on the platform and off.
 Taken out of context of the LinkedIn cinematic universe and shared on Twitter, these stories are a point of derision rather than admiration, and inspire parody more than parroting, underscoring the differences between LinkedIn and other social platforms.
   LinkedIn story-telling, driven by Rockstar Recruiters and other job provisioners, has evolved, as work has evolved. Browsing LinkedIn several years ago revealed the same line-by-line storytelling, but anecdotes that were thematically different. Then, the pendulum of storytelling swung toward hustle culture. Timelines were filled with team-building entrepreneurs doing their best Gary Vaynerchuk impression, imploring their followers to work more, try harder, and   eat shit to get ahead.
 Recruiters were gatekeepers who used LinkedIn to share the best ways to  lose access to them and get your resume thrown in the digital dustbin. While this brand still exists on the platform, it’s dwarfed by the new era of micro storytelling—empathy is trending. Now, the platform’s viral stories are about “bringing your whole self to work,” self-care, and finding work-life balance in a busy world. In light of how work has changed because of the pandemic, micro storytellers instruct us on having compassion for colleagues behind the screen, and the dangers of burnout with remote work.
 Given the accompanying economic downturn and the increased pressure when it comes to job searches, recruiters are selling HR as friends of the downtrodden, willing to look past less job experience and unbrushed hair when it comes to assessing candidates and giving them the coveted offer letter. In fact, they even prefer it! In the Land of LinkedIn, the young, hungry, and stupid are always better bets than the polished, professional, and competent. LinkedIn is filled with these stories about work –– real, exaggerated, and imagined.
  Reduced to its simplest form, LinkedIn is a digital resume. A profile consists of your past work experience, education, skills, and references. The posts, comments, and messages are like a cover letter. But we’ve long decided that there are better ways to showcase your ability than a list of the places you’ve worked, the school you went to, and a hastily drafted plea for work. Resumes are old scrolls of a bygone era. If LinkedIn is a site meant to demonstrate you’re an expert, it’s competing against all the places you can do this better.
 Developers have GitHub, designers use Dribbble, and Academics maintain their ResearchGate or Google Scholar profiles. There’s  Twitter, where an ethos of entertainment first, professionalism second makes “networking” more natural and even more effective. Plus, there’s personal websites and portfolios. Everyone is building a personal brand, they’re just not doing it on LinkedIn.
 The professional platform doesn’t quite work because it's a collection of contradictions. It's the site with millions of users that nobody you know uses. It’s where you put your resume, but never apply for a job.
 And yet, many of us have a profile and might even maintain it, adding a new role when we make a professional move. We may venture into InMail Infinity, not knowing whose invite to accept and in the end accepting them all. Some of us even “like” updates from promoted friends and former colleagues who are also performing professionalism, probably just as ambivalent about the platform as we are. Keeping our page current, skimming messages, and doing the very least to ensure our network doesn’t forget us is a “just-in-case” mentality powered by some level of professional anxiety.
 If you have a job, you  might lose it. If you don’t, you  might find one.
  LinkedIn is bizarre because it tries to make this hostage situation fun. Even though it’s not. Not when you add stories, audio messages, DMs, a social feed, or anything else. The platform might be less  alternate universe and more down to earth if the truth was acknowledged: performative professionalism, job hunting, and networking are extensions of  work not play. As long as LinkedIn pretends otherwise, we can also pretend that we’ll never be desperate enough to use it in earnest.
  Fadeke Adegbuyi is a senior marketing manager at Doist and a fan of all things internet culture.
title: LinkedIn是最糟糕的

在夜空中的一连串光线中，从遥远的小行星收集的样本在被日本太空探测器Hay鸟2号抛下后，于周日到达地球。
 科学家希望，这些珍贵的样品预计不超过0.1克物质，可以帮助阐明生命的起源和宇宙的形成。日本时间凌晨2点30分（格林尼治标准时间1730日星期六）之前，载有样品的胶囊进入大气层，当它进入地球大气层并进入澳大利亚的着陆点时，就产生了流星般的火球。
 ＆＃34;终于回到了地球，＆＃34;一位对抵达消息进行现场直播的官员说，图片显示日本航天局JAXA的官员兴奋地欢呼并挥舞着拳头。几个小时后，JAXA确认样品已被回收，并在胶囊从周六与Hayabusa-2分离后坠落到地球的胶囊发射的信标的帮助下，而冰箱大小的探测器则在220,000公里（137,000英里）左右。
 ＆＃34;我们找到了胶囊！连同降落伞！哇！该任务的Twitter帐户已宣布。聚集在东京郊区JAXA办公室附近的一个公共观看地点的人-尽管该事件发生在午夜后的几个小时-也在欢呼声中爆发。
   我非常高兴，因为胶囊安全地返回了家，Hay鸟2号做得很好，＆＃34;一个小学男孩说。该胶囊是在澳大利亚南部沙漠中回收的，现在将由科学家进行最初的非侵入性分析，包括检查任何气体排放。
 然后将其发送到日本。样品是由Hayabusa-2（于2014年发射的）从距地球约3亿公里的小行星Ryugu收集的。
 探针从表面下方收集表面灰尘和原始材料，然后通过烧制“撞击器”将其搅动。进入小行星。 
自从宇宙形成以来，这种材料就被认为是不变的。像地球这样的大天体经历了剧烈的变化，包括加热和凝固，从而改变了其表面和下方物质的成分。
 但是，当涉及到较小的行星或较小的小行星时，这些物质并未融化，因此，据信46亿年前的物质仍然存在，＆＃34; ab鸟二号任务负责人吉川诚告诉记者，胶囊到达之前。
 科学家特别热衷于发现样品中是否含有有机物，这可能有助于播种地球上的生命。
 我们仍然不知道地球上的生命起源和通过Hayabusa-2任务，如果我们能够研究和了解龙宫的这些有机材料，很可能这些有机材料就是来源地球上的生命吉川说。
 ＆＃34;我们之前从未有过这样的材料...水和有机物将受到研究，因此这是一个非常宝贵的机会，＆＃34;日本海洋地球科学技术局高级研究员Motoo Ito说。
 Hayabusa-2的一半样本将由JAXA，美国航天局NASA和其他国际组织共享，其余部分将留待日后研究，因为分析技术取得了进步。
   Hayabusa-2的工作尚未结束，Hayabusa-2现在将开始针对两个新小行星的扩展任务。 
在2026年接近第一个小行星2001 CC21之前，它将完成围绕太阳的一系列轨道，历时约六年。  该探测器的距离不会像龙宫那样近，但科学家希望它能够拍摄CC21，而且飞越将有助于发展有关如何保护地球免受小行星撞击的知识。  ab鸟2号将朝其主要目标1998 KY26前进，它是直径仅为30米的球形小行星。  它会观察并拍摄小行星，这并不是一件容易的事，因为它正在快速旋转，大约每10分钟绕其轴旋转一次。  但是Hayabusa-2不太可能着陆并收集样本，因为它可能没有足够的燃料将它们送回地球。
note_en:
In a streak of light across the night sky, samples collected from a distant asteroid arrived on Earth Sunday after being dropped off by Japanese space probe Hayabusa-2.
 Scientists hope the precious samples, which are expected to amount to no more than 0.1 grams of material, could help shed light on the origin of life and the formation of the universe.  The capsule carrying samples entered the atmosphere just before 2:30 am Japan time (1730 GMT Saturday), creating a shooting-star-like fireball as it entered Earth's atmosphere en route to a landing site in Australia.
 "Six years and it has finally come back to Earth," an official narrating a live broadcast of the arrival said, as images showed officials from Japan's space agency JAXA cheering and pumping their fists in excitement.   A few hours later, JAXA confirmed the samples had been recovered, with help from beacons emitted by the capsule as it plummeted to Earth after separating from Hayabusa-2 on Saturday, while the fridge-sized probe was some 220,000 kilometres (137,000 miles) away.
 "We found the capsule! Together with the parachute! Wow!" the mission's Twitter account announced.  People who had gathered at a public viewing site near JAXA's office in suburban Tokyo -- despite the event taking place a few hours after midnight -- also erupted with cheers.
   "I'm extremely happy because the capsule has returned home safely, Hayabusa-2 did a great job," a primary school boy said.  The capsule was recovered in the southern Australian desert, and will now be in the hands of scientists performing initial, non-invasive analysis including checking for any gas emissions.
 It will then be sent to Japan.    The samples were collected by Hayabusa-2, which launched in 2014, from the asteroid Ryugu, some 300 million kilometres from Earth.
 The probe collected both surface dust and pristine material from below the surface that was stirred up by firing an "impactor" into the asteroid.
 The material is believed to be unchanged since the time the universe was formed.  Larger celestial bodies like Earth went through radical changes including heating and solidifying, changing the composition of the materials on their surface and below.
 But "when it comes to smaller planets or smaller asteroids, these substances were not melted, and therefore it is believed that substances from 4.6 billion years ago are still there," Hayabusa-2 mission manager Makoto Yoshikawa told reporters before the capsule arrived.
 Scientists are especially keen to discover whether the samples contain organic matter, which could have helped seed life on Earth.
 "We still don't know the origin of life on Earth and through this Hayabusa-2 mission, if we are able to study and understand these organic materials from Ryugu, it could be that these organic materials were the source of life on Earth," Yoshikawa said.
 "We've never had materials like this before... water and organic matters will be subject to research, so this is a very valuable opportunity," said Motoo Ito, senior researcher at the Japan Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology.
 Half of Hayabusa-2's samples will be shared between JAXA, US space agency NASA and other international organisations, and the rest kept for future study as advances are made in analytic technology.
   The work is not over for Hayabusa-2, which will now begin an extended mission targeting two new asteroids.
 It will complete a series of orbits around the sun for around six years before approaching the first of the asteroids -- named 2001 CC21 -- in July 2026.
 The probe will not get as close as it did to Ryugu, but scientists hope it will be able to photograph CC21 and that the fly-by will help develop knowledge about how to protect Earth against asteroid impact.
 Hayabusa-2 will then head towards its main target, 1998 KY26, a ball-shaped asteroid with a diameter of just 30 metres.
  It will observe and photograph the asteroid, no easy task given that it is spinning rapidly, rotating on its axis about every 10 minutes.
 But Hayabusa-2 is unlikely to land and collect samples, as it probably would not have enough fuel to return them to Earth.

几年前，我学到了一种方便的技术来解决与不合作企业的纠纷。它易于理解且易于实施。您不需要律师或知名的社交媒体。它所需要的只是让您表现得像有组织的专业人员。最近，这项技术非常有效地解决了一个问题，我不得不分享这个故事。
 对话始于一位商人毫不犹豫地告诉我，他们拒绝支付欠我的钱：
       2017年，帕特里克·麦肯齐（Patrick McKenzie）写了一篇博客文章，题为“身份盗窃，信用报告和您”。表面上是关于对您的信用报告中的信息进行争议，但是这篇文章比Patrick所期望的更具笼统性。这对我展示了如何从战略上考虑组织的激励机制可以发挥多大的作用给我带来启发。
 在帕特里克（Patrick）的文章中，他解释说，银行会奖励其客户服务代理商，因为他们会尽快使客户下线。他们对合规团队每年减少银行监管事件的次数予以奖励。因此，帕特里克（Patrick）教他的读者如何从银行的客户服务部门导航到法律部门。从那里开始，客户将自己的权利充分了解，以使合规团队应该正确地担心法规事件。
 此策略更普遍地适用于企业。如果公司虐待您，通常该组织中的某人有权对其进行纠正。像任何人一样，潜在的问题解决者会遵循自我服务的动机。如果您能够识别出这些诱因并表现出影响它们的能力，那么该人很可能会认真对待您的问题。
 对我来说，最大的收获是帕特里克（Patrick）在整个争议期间保持口气的建议：
 …您希望通过某种方式与银行进行沟通，这表明您是有组织的专业人员，如果银行不自行处理，则有能力上报此事。您不会大喊[…]您不会大惊小怪[…]相反，您的出现就像您正在收集纸质记录一样。 
帕特里克（Patrick）的建议引起了我的共鸣，因为我通常不认为对人大喊大叫的价值，但我也不想掩盖卑鄙的商业行为。
  在过去的两年中，我经营了一个名为“ Is It Keto”的网站，该网站可以帮助初学者了解酮饮食。
   该网站随着时间的推移稳步增长，目前每月的网页浏览量超过100,000。这些数字足以吸引与其他酮类公司的直接合作。
 6月，我加入了一家名为Kiss My Keto的公司的会员计划。他们出售可食用酮类的面包和其他一些酮类食品。达成联盟交易意味着我要在Is It Keto上宣传他们的产品。如果有任何Is Is Keto读者单击“亲吻我的Keto”链接并进行购买，“亲吻我的Keto”将给我20％的佣金。
 Kiss My Keto的会员计划听起来不错，但是后来我才意识到这并不是看上去的样子。
  渴望看到我的收入猛增，我在开始为“亲吻我的酮”做广告后的第二天检查了我的会员统计。七个访问者单击了“亲吻我的Keto”链接，但我的“转化次数”仍然为零，这意味着我所推介的访问者中没有任何人购买任何东西。好的。也许价格比预期的要高，或者那七个都没有那天想要买东西的感觉。
 一个星期后，这是一回事。有26位用户访问过，但没有人购买任何东西。 
到一个月大关，我开始怀疑了。 Is It Keto的95位读者访问了Kiss My Keto，而会员统计数据仍声称我获得了零佣金。  就上下文而言，我与亚马逊有类似的关联交易，并且在同一时期单击“亚马逊”链接后，有17％的《 Is It Keto》读者进行了购买。  受到这些统计数据的困扰，我联系了Kiss My Keto的会员经理Nini Perez。  刚开始，她告诉我，也许我的客户出于好奇而只是单击链接：  它确实发生了。 有时，客户很好奇，他们去了网站，却离开而没有购买。  在其他时候，他们尝试购买不符合折扣条件的商品（订阅并保存，捆绑或面包）。  经过几次来回的摸索，我终于得到一个直截了当的答案，解释了我的佣金缺失： 
首先，Kiss My Keto已发布的政策明确规定，其佣金适用于所有产品，没有提及例外情况。或者至少他们当时做了。他们在上个月的某个时候删除了该页面，但我存档了副本。
   其次，面包是Kiss My Keto的旗舰产品。如果您访问他们的网站，他们会特别展示面包，以至于您认为他们是面包店。面包的一个隐藏例外就是，如果Zappos在其所有产品上广告了一个佣金，然后又说：“哦，但是我们不是说鞋子。”
   而且，这甚至还没有涉及到Nini电子邮件的“促销活动”部分。几天前，“亲吻我的Keto”向其会员发送了一封电子邮件，鼓励他们通过共享“亲吻我的Keto”在整个网站的7月4日的折扣代码来“提升其收入”。
   根据Nini的说法，即使客户通过会员链接购买了商品，Kiss My Keto也会对使用该优惠券的任何交易保留佣金。甚至在特价促销之外，当您访问Kiss My Keto时，他们的网站也会用不同的折扣向您报价：
   根据Nini所说，这些特别优惠与该会员的推荐信用竞争。如果客户选择了新的折扣，则Kiss My Keto会向首先将客户带到该站点的会员支付zilch费用。
  对我而言，“亲吻我的Keto”的发布价格与其实际支出之间的差异似乎具有误导性，甚至可能构成欺诈。 Nini提出使用Kiss My Keto产品补偿我，“所以[我]可以创建一篇新文章，然后看看它的进展。”但这显然不是我们的同意。是时候采用有组织的专业方法了。
 我将以下电子邮件发送给Kiss My Keto的首席执行官兼联合创始人Alex Bird： 
Nini与我合作时一直专业且礼貌，但她目前所描述的“亲吻我的基多”政策是不可接受的，因此，我正在与您联系以解决这个问题。
 根据引荐统计数据，我已经通过引荐链接吸引了120位访问者访问“亲吻我的Keto”。令我惊讶的是，在吸引了如此众多的访问者之后，我没有获得任何转化。
 当我问妮妮这件事时，她说“亲吻我的基托”是根据不属于已发布的大使协议一部分的例外而扣留款项的。具体来说，Nini向我报告了以下未发布的例外情况：
  Kiss My Keto大使页面上指出，会员计划支付所有Kiss My Keto产品销售额的20％。
 这些例外也直接与“亲吻我的Keto”发送的消息相矛盾。 […] 7月2日，阿莱克·姆瓦利（Alec Mwali）发送了一封电子邮件，鼓励他们分享“亲吻我的Keto” 7月4日的折扣代码，以“提升[我们的]收入”。
 Kiss My Keto欠您的网站是Is It Keto，这是Is It Keto的引荐链接产生的，该网站上所有商定的客户订单中，已同意的20％。这笔钱归功于Is It Keto，无论客户购买了何种产品或采用的折扣代码，因为已发布的协议均规定收取20％的佣金，无一例外。
 我正在执行电子邮件中的一些操作，以遵循“有组织的专业方法”： 
在可能的情况下，我会使用我们公司的名称，而不是说“您”或“我”。  两周后，Alex仍然没有回应，因此我向他发送了此后续邮件：  2020年7月17日，我未收到您的回信，该信是关于“亲吻我的Keto”未付给我的广告佣金的。  如果我在2020年8月7日星期五之前仍未收到答复，我将假定“亲吻我的酮”不愿履行大使协议，并且我将寻求其他手段来追回所欠款项。  我暗示采取法律行动，但未明确威胁要采取法律行动。 如果他们没有付款，我的计划是向联邦贸易委员会投诉，并联系其他关联公司，建议他们这样做。  “坦率地说，我们不会为您的转化而付钱给您”🔗︎  我们的大使经理Nini告诉我，我们已经为您支付了最新的转化费用。 
现在，有人告诉我您仍然欠欠无法正确跟踪的旧广告系列。坦率地说，我们不付您转换费用。
 这是迈克尔（Michael）的事情……我们无法根据您认为自己应得的转化次数从帽子中抽出任意数字。
 妮妮（Nini）谈到了引荐，他们告诉她95次点击中的0次转化并非没有可能，因此看来您的点击量根本没有转化。
 您的会员链接可能无效吗？当然，一切皆有可能，但是推荐保证我们可以正常工作，因此我认为这种情况极不可能。
 您是否有未跟踪的转化证明？如果您愿意，请在这里与我分享，我们可以解决。我们将跟进这些客户，以深入调查此事，当然，我们将相应地向您付款。
 关于Herscu的电子邮件，有一些令人困惑的地方需要我打开。
 他说：“我们已经付给您了。”我不知道，“亲吻我的酮”自发地开始向我支付购买面包的佣金（以下更多内容）。在Herscu回应我的前一天，我收到了PayPal付款，金额为$ 26.60，这是我在前两周获得的佣金。 
Herscu似乎也误解了真正的问题。他的举动似乎很简单，这就是不信任Kiss My Keto的电话号码的问题。尽管我确实对他们的数量表示怀疑，但是我不想参加这场战斗，因为我没有证据证明我的任何读者都购买了东西。我可以证明的是，“亲吻我的凯托”的广告薪酬结构与他们的会员经理私下向我描述的情况有所不同。
  问题与推荐的统计数据无关，而与“亲吻我的Keto”的政策有关。
 Kiss My Keto已发布的大使文件指出，Kiss My Keto支付“所有Kiss My Keto产品销售的起始佣金的20％。”
 Nini Perez在7月16日给我的电子邮件中说，这些条款有例外。妮妮（Nini）特别指出，“亲吻我的Keto”会扣减销售“ Kiss My Keto”整个网站期间的面包产品或购买的佣金。
 Nini向我报告的信息准确吗？如果是这样，该信息在哪里发布？我可以找到的所有官方文件都说，所有销售的收益都是20％。
 我故意将谈话的内容从统计数据中移开，因为我无法证明“ Kiss My Keto”的内部销售数字有多少，尽管看起来可能令人怀疑。通过提出已发布的政策的问题，我将对话强迫到了我们共享访问相同信息的地方。
     那是一个好兆头。他问他能做些什么来使我停止打扰他，所以他似乎可以解决。 
如果根据已发布的大使协议，如果Kiss My Keto支付Is It Keto的全部佣金，我将认为此事已解决。
 在6月16日至7月16日之间，References的统计数据显示，Is It Keto将120位访问者推荐给Kiss My Keto，并获得了0转换的功劳。我希望“亲吻我的Keto”将根据“亲吻我的Keto”未发布的例外排除的所有转化支付全部20％的佣金。
 如果Kiss My Keto难以以这种方式查看购买历史记录，我会接受$ 88作为我欠的佣金的合理估算。该数字基于以下事实：自7月17日以来，我在36个推荐中获得了26.60美元的收入，即每次推荐0.73美元。将其推断为120位访问者可获得$ 88.66。
     您可能已经注意到我对《亲吻我的酮》的要求存在逻辑上的矛盾。我最初的问题是，“亲吻我的Keto”的统计数据似乎不可信，所以为什么我突然在7月16日之后将所有内容都视为可靠？
 7月16日发生了一些变化，这对我非常有利。突然，“亲吻我的凯托”开始将面包销售归功于我，他们私下声称不符合佣金资格。有趣的是，我的第一笔佣金是在给他们的首席执行官亚历克斯·伯德（Alex Bird）发送电子邮件不到24小时后发生的：
   这对我来说是个好消息，因为它给了我很多数字，可以推断出我的失踪销售额。当我的佣金为零时，Kiss My Keto可以简单地说，来自我网站的所有客户都没有完成购买。如果没有他们的内部收据，我将没有证据可辩。通过增加面包的佣金，他们使我了解了我的佣金在整个期间应该是什么样子。
 自从进行电子邮件交换以来，我收集了更多数据，现在他们在7月16日之前的帐目似乎更加令人怀疑： 
Kiss My Keto是否付钱给我，是因为我说服了他们这是符合道德和正义的事情？可能不会。
 在我看来，“亲吻我的Keto”违反了他们公布的政策，我有明确的证据，88美元似乎让我感到安心。在给首席执行官发送电子邮件之后，他们删除了会员政策页面，当我推荐的客户购买面包时，我开始收取佣金。
 即使Kiss My Keto认为我的主张是毫无根据的，并付了我88美元只是为了让我嗡嗡作响，但这仍然是一场胜利。如果我喘不过气来，告诉他们我有多生气，他们只会停止回应。 Herscu开始时说：“坦率地说，我们不付钱给您。”两封电子邮件之后，我把他带到：“ 88美元对我来说似乎很公平。”即使我所做的只是说服他，88美元的价格比应付我可能造成的任何麻烦都便宜，但有组织的专业方法给人留下了深刻的印象。
 我经常使用这种方法。有时它可以奏效，有时却不奏效，但我始终认为这是解决争议的压力最小的方法。当公司进行不良行为时，我会想到以下问题：
 如何向他们证明我的组织能力和维护自己权利的能力？
  “身份盗用，信用报告和您”：Patrick McKenzie的文章向我介绍了这些技术
note_en:
A few years ago, I learned a handy technique for resolving disputes with uncooperative businesses. It’s simple to understand and easy to implement. You don’t need lawyers or a prominent social media presence. All it requires is for you to behave like an organized professional. This technique recently resolved a problem so effectively that I had to share the story.
 The conversation began with a merchant telling me in no uncertain terms that they refused to pay the money they owed me:
       In 2017, Patrick McKenzie wrote a blog post called  “Identity Theft, Credit Reports, and You." It’s ostensibly about disputing information on your credit report, but the article is more generalizable than Patrick perhaps intended. It was an illuminating read for me in demonstrating how much power you can wield by thinking strategically about an organization’s incentives.
 In Patrick’s article, he explains that banks reward their customer service agents for getting customers off the phone as quickly as possible. They reward their compliance team for minimizing the bank’s number of regulatory incidents per year. Therefore, Patrick taught his readers how to navigate from a bank’s customer service department to their legal department. From there, the customer presents themselves as knowledgeable enough of their rights that the compliance team should rightly fear a regulatory incident.
 This strategy applies to businesses more generally. If a company mistreats you, usually someone at the organization has the power to correct it. The potential problem solver follows self-serving incentives, as anyone does. If you can identify those incentives and demonstrate an ability to affect them, then the person is likely to take your problem seriously.
 The biggest takeaway for me was Patrick’s recommendations on the tone to maintain throughout the dispute:
 …you want to communicate with the bank in a manner which suggests that you’re an organized professional who is capable of escalating the matter if the bank does not handle it themselves. You do not yell […] You do not bluster […] You instead present as if you’re collecting a paper trail.
 Patrick’s recommendation resonated with me because I generally don’t see value in yelling at people, but I also don’t want to take shady business practices lying down.
  For the past two years, I’ve run a site called  Is It Keto that helps beginners navigate the keto diet.
   The site has grown steadily over time, to the point where it now has over 100,000 pageviews per month. Those numbers are high enough to attract direct partnerships with other keto companies.
 In June, I joined the affiliate program for a company called Kiss My Keto. They sell keto-friendly bread and a few other keto foods. The affiliate deal meant that I’d advertise their products on Is It Keto. If any Is It Keto readers clicked a Kiss My Keto link and made a purchase, Kiss My Keto would give me a 20% commission.
 Kiss My Keto’s affiliate program sounded like a good deal, but I would later come to realize it was not what it seemed.
  Eager to see my earnings skyrocket, I checked my affiliate stats the day after I began advertising Kiss My Keto. Seven visitors had clicked links to Kiss My Keto, but I still had zero “conversions,” meaning that none of the visitors I referred had purchased anything. Okay, fine. Maybe prices were higher than they expected, or none of those seven felt like buying anything that day.
 After a week, it was the same thing. 26 users had visited, but none of them purchased anything.
   By the one month mark, I became suspicious. 95 readers from Is It Keto had visited Kiss My Keto, and the affiliate stats still claimed I had earned zero commissions.
 For context, I had a similar affiliate deal with Amazon, and 17% of Is It Keto readers made a purchase after clicking an Amazon link in the same period.
  Baffled by these stats, I reached out to Nini Perez, Kiss My Keto’s affiliate manager.
  At first, she told me that maybe my customers just click the links out of curiosity:
 It does happen. Sometimes, customers are curious and go to the site but leave without purchasing.
  Other times, they try to purchase an item not qualified for a discount (subscribe & save, bundles or bread).
  After a few back and forths, I finally got a straight answer that explained my missing commissions:
    First, Kiss My Keto’s published policies say explicitly that their commissions apply to  all products and make no mention of exceptions. Or at least they did at the time. They removed that page sometime in the last month, but I  archived a copy.
   Secondly, bread is Kiss My Keto’s flagship product. If you visit their website, they showcase bread so prominently that you’d think they’re a bread store. A hidden exception for bread would be like if  Zappos advertised a commission on all their products and then later said, “Oh, but we didn’t mean  shoes.”
   And that’s not even getting into the “sale event” part of Nini’s email. Days earlier, Kiss My Keto sent their affiliates an email encouraging them to “boost [their] earnings” by sharing Kiss My Keto’s sitewide 4th of July discount codes.
   According to Nini, Kiss My Keto withheld commissions on any transaction that used that coupon, even if the customer purchased through an affiliate link. Even outside of special sales, when you visit Kiss My Keto, their site bombards you with offers for different discounts:
   Based on what Nini said, these special offers compete against the affiliate’s referral credit. If the customer chooses a new discount, Kiss My Keto pays zilch to the affiliate who brought the customer to the site in the first place.
  To me, the discrepancy between Kiss My Keto’s published rates and their actual payouts seemed misleading and maybe even constituted fraud. Nini offered to compensate me instead with Kiss My Keto products, “so [I] can create a new article and let’s see how it goes.” But that was clearly not our agreement. It was time to employ The Organized Professional Method.
 I sent the following email to Alex Bird, Kiss My Keto’s CEO and co-founder:
 Nini has been professional and courteous in working with me, but she is currently describing a Kiss My Keto policy that is unacceptable, so I’m reaching out to you to address it.
 According to Refersion stats, I’ve driven 120 visitors to Kiss My Keto through my referral links. I was surprised to see that after this many visitors, I had not been credited with any conversions.
 When I asked Nini about this, she said that Kiss My Keto withholds payments based on exceptions that are not part of the published ambassador agreement. Specifically, Nini reported to me the following unpublished exceptions:
  The Kiss My Keto ambassador page states that the affiliate program  pays 20% on sales of all Kiss My Keto products.
 These exceptions also directly contradict messaging from Kiss My Keto. […] On July 2nd,  Alec Mwali sent out an email encouraging ambassadors to “boost [our] earnings” by sharing Kiss My Keto’s 4th of July discount codes.
 Kiss My Keto owes Is It Keto the agreed upon 20% of all customer orders to your site that originated from Is It Keto’s referral link. This money is due to Is It Keto regardless of the product the customer purchased or the discount code that they applied, as the published agreement states a commission of 20% with no exceptions.
 I’m doing a few things in the email to adhere to The Organized Professional Method:
 Wherever possible, I used our businesses’ names instead of saying “you” or “me.”
 After two weeks, Alex still hadn’t responded, so I sent him this follow-up:
 I have not received a response from you in response to my email dated July 17th, 2020 regarding Kiss My Keto’s failure to pay me its advertised commissions.
 If I do not receive a response by Friday, August 7th, 2020, I will assume that Kiss My Keto is not willing to honor the ambassador agreement, and I will seek other means to recover the money owed.
  I  hint at legal action but don’t explicitly threaten it. If they failed to pay, my plan was to file a complaint with the  Federal Trade Commission and reach out to other affiliates suggesting they do the same.
 “To put it blatantly, we are not paying you for your conversions”  🔗︎
  Nini, our ambassador manager tells me we have already paid you for the most recent conversions.
 Now I’m being told you feel you are still owed for older campaigns that weren’t tracked properly. To put it blatantly, we are not paying you for your conversions.
 Here[sic] the thing Michael… We can’t pull an arbitrary number out of a hat based on a number of conversions that you feel you were owed.
 Nini spoke to Refersion and they told her 0 conversions out of 95 clicks is not impossible so it seems your traffic simply did not convert.
 Is it possible your affiliate link wasn’t working? Sure, anything is possible but Refersion assures us it’s working so I think it’s extremely unlikely this was the case.
 Do you have any proof of conversions that weren’t tracked? If you do please share them here with me and we can address them. We will follow up with those customers to get to the bottom of the matter and of course, we will pay you out accordingly.
 There are a few things that are confusing about Herscu’s email that I need to unpack.
 He says, “we have already paid you.” Unbeknownst to me, Kiss My Keto had spontaneously begun paying me commissions on bread purchases (more on that  below). The day before Herscu responded to me, I received a PayPal payment for $26.60 for commissions I earned over the preceding two weeks.
 Herscu also seems to misunderstand the real issue. He was acting as though it was a simple matter of distrusting Kiss My Keto’s numbers. Though I was indeed skeptical of their numbers, I didn’t want to fight that battle because I had no evidence to prove any of my readers purchased something. What I could prove was that Kiss My Keto’s advertised pay structure differed from what their affiliate manager described to me privately.
  The issue is not with Refersion’s stats but with Kiss My Keto’s policy.
 Kiss My Keto’s published Ambassador documentation states that Kiss My Keto pays “20% starting commission on sales of all Kiss My Keto products.”
 On July 16th, Nini Perez stated in an email to me that there are exceptions to these terms. Specifically, Nini said that Kiss My Keto withholds commissions on sales of bread products or purchases made during a Kiss My Keto sitewide sale.
 Is the information that Nini reported to me accurate? If so, where is that information published? All official documentation I can find says that the payout is 20% on all sales.
 I intentionally steered the conversation away from stats, as I had no way to prove anything about Kiss My Keto’s internal sales numbers, however dubious they might seem. By posing the question of published policies, I forced the conversation to a place where we share access to the same information.
     That was a good sign. He asked what he can do to make me stop bothering him, so he appeared amenable to a solution.
  I will consider this matter resolved if Kiss My Keto pays Is It Keto full commission based on the published Ambassador agreement.
 Between June 16th and July 16th, Refersion’s statistics show that Is It Keto referred 120 visitors to Kiss My Keto and received credit for 0 conversions. I expect Kiss My Keto to pay the full 20% commission on any conversions that were excluded based on Kiss My Keto’s unpublished exceptions.
 If it’s difficult for Kiss My Keto to review the purchase history in that way, I would accept $88 as a fair estimate of my owed commissions. This number is based on the fact that I’ve earned $26.60 on 36 referrals since July 17th, which is $0.73 per referral. Extrapolating that to 120 visitors yields $88.66.
     You may have noticed a logical inconsistency in my demand to Kiss My Keto. My original issue was that Kiss My Keto’s statistics seemed untrustworthy, so why was I suddenly treating everything after July 16th as reliable?
 Something changed on July 16th that was very much in my favor. Suddenly, Kiss My Keto started crediting me for sales of bread, the thing they privately had claimed was ineligible for commission. Interestingly, my first commission occurred less than 24 hours after I emailed their CEO, Alex Bird:
   That was great news for me because it gave me hard numbers to extrapolate my missing sales. When I had zero commissions, Kiss My Keto could simply claim that none of the customers who came from my site completed purchases. Without access to their internal receipts, I’d have no evidence to argue. By flipping on commissions for bread, they gave me a view into what my commissions  should have looked like the entire time.
 Since the email exchange, I’ve collected more data, and now their accounting prior to July 16th seems even more suspect:
    Did Kiss My Keto pay me because I convinced them that it was the ethical and just thing to do? Probably not.
 To me, it looks like Kiss My Keto was violating their published policies, I had clear evidence of it, and $88 seemed like a cheap price to placate me. After my email to the CEO, they took down  their affiliate policy page, and I started receiving commissions when customers I referred purchased bread.
 Even if Kiss My Keto thought my assertions were baseless and paid me $88 just to get me to buzz off, that’s still a win. Had I huffed and puffed and told them how mad I was, they would have simply stopped responding. Herscu started at, “to put it blatantly, we’re not paying you.” Two emails later, I brought him to, “$88 seems fair to me.” Even if all I did was convince him that $88 was cheaper than dealing with whatever nuisance I might cause, The Organized Professional Method created that impression.
 I use this method frequently. Sometimes it works, and sometimes it doesn’t, but I always find it to be the least stressful way of addressing disputes. When a company engages in bad behavior, I think about these questions:
 How can I demonstrate to them that I’m organized and capable of asserting my rights?
  “Identity Theft, Credit Reports, and You”: Patrick McKenzie’s article that taught me these techniques
title: 我如何从不道德的商人那里收债
title_en: How I Collected a Debt from an Unscrupulous Merchant

note:
从1969年到1971年，美国在阿拉斯加的一个阿留申群岛（Aleutian Islands）中测试核武器，这令人担忧地靠近世界上尚存的少数水獭。地下爆炸促使养护管理人员执行一项大胆的计划。
 首先，他们捕捞了一些阿拉斯加海獭。然后他们在华盛顿州海岸附近释放了59人，在俄勒冈州附近释放了93人。这既是救援任务，又是返乡。在毛皮贸易商把它们捕杀到灭绝的边缘之前，海獭曾经在北美的太平洋海岸上来回摆动，吞噬着海胆，并帮助维护着挥舞着的海带塔。
 在华盛顿，进行了移植。但是几年之内，俄勒冈州的水獭就消失了。 “最大的问题是：俄勒冈州发生了什么事？”西雅图水族馆的保护生物学家肖恩·拉森（Shawn Larson）说，因为答案可以为移植工作提供参考。
 一种假设认为水獭只是游走，向北走。但是，在2008年提出的另一种解释则表明，适应冷水的阿拉斯加人之所以灭绝，是因为它们与俄勒冈州最初的皮草贸易商大相径庭。科学家将水獭分为北部和南部亚种，每个亚种都适应其自身的栖息地。如果该州古老的水獭居民与南部亚种有更密切的联系，那么也许在那段沿海地区重新引入海獭的工作应改为与加利福尼亚种群的动物一起工作。
 从人类考古学借来的技术可以回答这个保护问题。星期三在皇家学会B会刊上发表的新研究表明，北部亚种早已在俄勒冈海岸定居。
 “我们的建议是，实际上阿拉斯加或其他北方人口是合适的，”俄勒冈大学的人类学研究生Hannah Wellman说。该研究汇编了俄勒冈州原始海獭迄今为止最全面的遗传数据集。
 为了了解曾经生活过哪些水獭，研究小组转向了俄勒冈北部两个与Clatsop和Tillamook群体相关的考古遗址中发现的水獭牙齿。他们的目标是从标本中提取基因序列。 
这些水獭是在距欧洲接触和海上皮草贸易相距很远的一千年前就被猎杀或交易的，分析结果表明了这一点。分子人类学家考特尼·霍夫曼（Courtney Hofman）说：“我们在两个相邻地点的遗传多样性比现代海獭要多得多，”他是从俄克拉荷马大学的牙齿中提取古代DNA的。
 为了补充这一发现，研究小组还与华盛顿特区的史密森学会（Smithsonian）接触了一些稀有的海獭标本，这些标本是水獭日渐稀少时毛皮贸易日渐衰落的日子。
 由于禁止进行任何会破坏样本的分析，研究人员决定让保存完好的水獭像牙医一样拜访。他们从牙齿上刮下了牙结石，然后小心地从该牙结石中提取了小比例的水獭DNA。
 结合先前的研究，两个时期的基因组序列表明，北部和南部的水獭种群曾经被称为俄勒冈州的海岸线之家。拉森博士说，也许该地区曾经是一个遗传途径，可以在太平洋沿岸上下繁殖。这意味着可以将阿拉斯加水獭用于重新引进计划。
 韦尔曼女士说，她计划与Elakha联盟共享她的数据，该联盟是一个非营利组织，由Siletz印第安人同盟部落和Coquille印第安部落支持。该小组的目标是永久性地带回俄勒冈州的水獭以及它们所栖息的高耸的海带森林。
 今年2月，美国鱼类和野生动物服务局（US.Fish and Wildlife Service）资助了Elakha联盟进行的可行性研究，这是一系列评估和步骤中的第一项研究，最终可能使俄勒冈州的海獭重获新生。
 正在协助撰写评估报告的拉尔森博士说，她计划将俄勒冈州过去水獭多样性的新分析纳入其中。重新引进也许可以将加利福尼亚州的水獭带入该州的南部海岸，而更北部的阿拉斯加水獭也可以带入。 
她说：“然后，随着人口的增长，他们将合并。现在，我们将拥有一个从俄勒冈州南部一直延伸到俄罗斯的连接团体。” “那会很棒。”
note_en:
From 1969 to 1971, the United States was testing nuclear weapons beneath one of Alaska’s Aleutian Islands, worryingly close to some of the world’s few surviving sea otters. The subterranean explosions prompted conservation managers to carry out a daring plan.
 First, they netted some Alaskan sea otters. Then they set 59 free off the coast of Washington State and 93 more near Oregon. This was part rescue mission, part homecoming. Before fur traders hunted them to the brink of extinction, sea otters used to bob and roll up and down North America’s Pacific Coast,  gobbling down sea urchins and helping to maintain waving towers of kelp.
 In Washington, the transplants took. But within a few years the Oregon otters vanished. “The biggest question is: What happened to Oregon?” said Shawn Larson, a conservation biologist at the Seattle Aquarium, because the answer could inform transplantation efforts.
 One hypothesis holds that the otters simply swam off, heading back north. But another explanation,  advanced in 2008, suggests that the cold water-adapted Alaskans died out because they were too different from Oregon’s original pre-fur trade otters. Scientists divide sea otters into northern and southern subspecies, each adapted to its own habitat. If the state’s ancient otter inhabitants were more closely related to the southern subspecies, maybe sea otter reintroduction efforts along that stretch of coast should instead work with animals from Californian populations.
 A technique borrowed from the science of human archaeology may answer this conservation question. New research  published Wednesday in Proceedings of the Royal Society B suggests that the northern subspecies had long settled along the Oregon coast.
 “Our recommendations are that actually Alaskan or other northern populations would be appropriate,” said Hannah Wellman, a graduate student in anthropology at the University of Oregon who led the study. The research compiled the most comprehensive genetic data set yet of Oregon’s original sea otters.
 To understand which otters had once lived there, the team turned to otter teeth found in two archaeological sites in northern Oregon associated with the Clatsop and Tillamook groups. Their goal was to extract genetic sequences from the specimens.
  These otters were hunted or traded well over a thousand years ago, long before European contact and the maritime fur trade, and the results of the analysis show it. “We have a lot more genetic diversity in these two adjacent sites than we do in modern sea otters,” said Courtney Hofman, a molecular anthropologist who extracted ancient DNA from the teeth at the University of Oklahoma.
 To complement that finding, the team also reached out to the Smithsonian in Washington, D.C. for a few rare sea otter specimens from the waning days of the fur trade when otters became increasingly scarce.
 Barred from any analysis that would destroy the samples, the researchers settled on giving those preserved otters the equivalent of a dentist visit. They scraped calculus off the teeth, then carefully extracted the small percentage of otter DNA in that calculus.
 Combined with previous studies, genomic sequences from both time periods show that both northern and southern sea otter populations once called Oregon’s coastline home. Perhaps the region was once a genetic way station, allowing interbreeding up and down the Pacific Coast, says Dr. Larson, who did not participate in the current work. And that implies that Alaskan otters can be used for reintroduction programs.
 Ms. Wellman says she plans to share her data with the  Elakha Alliance, a nonprofit organization supported by the Confederated Tribes of Siletz Indians and the Coquille Indian Tribe. The group’s goal is to bring back Oregon’s otters — and the towering kelp forests they tend — for good.
 This past February, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service funded a feasibility study by the Elakha Alliance, the first in a long series of evaluations and steps that could ultimately bring back Oregon’s sea otters.
 Dr. Larson, who is helping write that assessment, says she plans to incorporate the new analysis of Oregon’s past otter diversity. Perhaps reintroductions can bring in both Californian sea otters to the state’s southern shores, and Alaskan otters farther north.
 “Then as the populations grow, they’ll merge, and now we’ll have a connected group that is basically continuous from southern Oregon all the way up to Russia,” she said. “Which would be awesome.”

 从臭名昭著的丝绸之路开始，暗网市场就一直吸引着公众，在2013年鼎盛时期，它占据了所有比特币活动的近20％。此后，尽管它们现在在整体加密货币活动中所占的比例要小得多，市场生态系统显着增长。在2019年，我们发现整个暗网市场实现了价值近8亿美元的加密货币收入，该年度有49个独立市场活跃。
 但是，2020年则是另一回事。虽然暗网市场总收入已经超过了2019年的总收入，但购买的总数以及可能的客户总数已经大大下降，尽管其余的购买是为了获得更高的价值。同样，活跃市场的数量也大大减少，有几个著名的市场关闭了，而出现的新市场却很少出现。
 为什么会这样呢？有人可能会认为持续的Covid危机是显而易见的答案。正如我们将在下面进行探讨的那样，大流行确实使全世界的邮政系统紧张，导致许多暗网市场供应商的递送失败和延误。但是，我们与之交谈的专家认为，Covid不应归咎于今年的大量市场关闭。取而代之的是，日益加剧的竞争加上执法的努力正在使暗网市场生态系统整合为一些大参与者-这是技术行业和其他合法和非法市场的共同模式。下面，我们将分享我们对2020年暗网市场活动的发现，在整个大流行中的变化以及可能导致许多市场关闭的原因。
  今年早些时候，大约在美国开始封锁的三周之后，我们检查了大流行对暗网市场活动的影响，发现暗网市场交易活动在比特币和其他加密货币价格急剧下跌之后有所下降。
  在我们的发现中值得注意的是，到目前为止，暗网市场活动似乎不受比特币市场活动的影响。比特币价值的波动一直很普遍，很少出现在暗网市场消费者的购买活动中。但是，当比特币的价格在美国第一轮锁定之后于3月中旬开始下跌时，暗网市场活动也随之而来。
 但是事实证明，这种改变只是暂时的。从5月左右开始，暗网市场收入恢复到之前的状态，不再与比特币的价格同步变动。自那时以来，暗网市场的每月收入一直稳定增长，除了9月和11月的小幅下降外，这在很大程度上与季节性趋势保持一致。 
凭借这些最新发展，2020年的整个暗网市场收入已超过2019年，仅剩一个月了。但是尽管总收入可能不会改变，但其他数字表明，暗网市场可能会面临艰难时期。
  上图显示了按年计算的暗网市场总收入以及向暗网市场转移的总数，我们可以使用这些粗略数来粗略估计单个客户和购买的数量。有趣的是，我们看到虽然收入已经超过了2019年的总和（再次，还有一个月的余地），但向暗网市场的转移总额仅略高于900万，远低于达到2019年的总和超过1200万的速度。数字显示，与2019年相比，2020年的客户购买量减少，但每次购买的金额更大。这可能表明，临时购买者或购买个人用途药物的人正在从暗网市场转移，而购买数量较大的人-要么个人使用或出售给他人-正在购买更多商品。这也可能意味着在不确定的情况下，一些临时买家已经开始下达更大的订单以库存。
 我们还看到2020年更多的暗网市场关闭，包括Flugsvamp 2.0和Empire等著名市场。我们在下表中看到了这一点，该图表显示了自2015年1月以来每个月的活跃市场数量（活跃意味着该市场在给定月份内至少收到价值100美元的加密货币）。
  尽管一些市场声称其关闭只是暂时的，但2020年11月活跃的37个暗网市场是自2017年11月以来的最低水平。我们在2019年没有看到这种下降。实际上，今年活跃市场的下降是在2010年持续增长之后从2018年到2020年2月的活跃市场数量。
  通常很难说出为什么市场会在关闭时关闭，因为管理员经常撤出出口骗局，在这种骗局中，市场停止运作，但在公开场合似乎仍然活跃，以便管理员可以继续从永远无法实现的采购中筹集资金。其他市场已成为来自其他市场的拒绝服务（DoS）攻击的受害者，在某些情况下显然是关闭的。我们在Empire Market这个案例中看到了这两种现象，Empire Market是一个广受人们信任的大型暗网市场，其运营商在受到DoS攻击两天后于今年早些时候退出了骗局。
  Covid无疑会引起供应链中断，尤其是运输延迟，从而阻碍了暗网市场的销售和运营。暗网市场观察者已经在诸如Dread之类的以暗网市场为中心的论坛上以客户投诉的形式看到了这种情况，并在卖方为买家设定期望的注释中看到了这一点。
   证据也不只是轶事。犯罪学研究人员AndréanneBergeron，DavidDécary-Hétu和Luca Giommoni最近发表了一项研究，分析了Covid禁售开始在美国和欧洲之前和之后销售的数百种暗网市场药品，以确定该病毒对业务的影响程度。他们发现，在Covid之前的2020年1月1日至3月21日之间，任何给定日期的所有订单中有60％至100％成功。但是，在Covid锁定开始之后，该研究发现，只有21％的交付按时成功完成。因此，客户和供应商指责Covid的交货时间较长是正确的。 
但是运输延误和其他与Covid相关的运营困难是否导致市场关闭？我们跟进了该研究的两名研究者讲师AndréanneBergeron和DavidDécary-Hétu教授，征求他们的意见。他们重申自己的观点，即Covid通过对邮政服务施加更大的压力而导致了持续的暗网市场交付延迟。 “世界还没有恢复正常，因此市场尚未纠正自己也就不足为奇了。邮政服务做得不好。” Bergeron说。
 然而，研究人员并不认为2020年暗网市场的关闭是Covid的直接结果。 “运行暗网市场比以往任何时候都更具挑战性–您必须启用安全性并防范DoS攻击，然后再进行竞争。所有这些因素限制了药物的可获得性。”Décary-Hétu说。他认为，竞争的这些自然力量，而不是Covid危机，是关闭率提高的真正原因，并指出Chainalysis数据可以说明这一点。
 “不包括Hydra，如果所有的暗网市场每年赚取2.5亿美元，而管理员收取5％的佣金，则这1250万美元的总和除以必须支付很多员工的所有市场。 Décary-Hétu说：“不值得再坐100年以上的牢狱之灾。”
  暗网市场似乎在2020年处于不稳定状态，一些市场关闭，其余市场则依赖不断缩水的客户来获取收入。违反直觉，尽管影响发货时间，但Covid似乎不是这些问题的主要原因。相反，暗网市场整合可能是该类别自身特有的竞争力的结果，而Covid最多只是简单地加快了已经存在的趋势。
 我们在所谓的“赢家通吃”市场（如技术）中看到类似的动态竞争，随着时间的推移，竞争自然会使市场萎缩到最大，最高效的参与者。暗网市场与科技公司之间当然存在关键区别-例如，苹果公司不必担心被执法部门关闭。但是，正如Décary-Hétu教授指出的那样，暗网市场是一项艰巨的业务，而且越来越少的市场表明，并非今天站立的人都能幸免。

This blog is an excerpt from the Chainalysis 2021 Crypto Crime Report.    Click here   to download the full document!
 Darknet markets have long fascinated the public, starting with the infamous Silk Road, which accounted for  nearly 20% of all Bitcoin activity during its heyday in 2013. Since then, though they now account for a much smaller percent of overall cryptocurrency activity, the darknet market ecosystem has grown significantly. In 2019,  we found that darknet markets as a whole took in nearly $800 million worth of cryptocurrency in revenue, with 49 individual markets active during the year.
 However, 2020 has been a different story. While total darknet market revenue has already surpassed 2019 totals, the overall number of purchases, and likely customers as well, has fallen significantly, though the remaining purchases are for higher values. Similarly, the number of active markets has fallen significantly, with several prominent ones shutting down and fewer new ones popping up to take their place.
 Why is this happening? One might think the ongoing Covid crisis is the obvious answer. As we’ll explore below, the pandemic has indeed strained postal systems around the world, leading to delivery failures and delays for many darknet market vendors. But the experts we spoke to don’t think that Covid is to blame for this year’s rash of market closures. Instead, it appears that ever-increasing competition combined with the efforts of law enforcement are causing the darknet market ecosystem to consolidate to a few big players — a pattern common to the technology industry and other markets, both legal and illegal. Below, we’ll share our findings on darknet market activity in 2020, how it’s changed throughout the pandemic, and provide possible reasons why so many markets have closed.
  Earlier this year, roughly three weeks after lockdowns began in the United States, we  examined the pandemic’s effects on darknet market activity and found that darknet market transaction activity had dropped following a sharp decline in the price of Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies.
  Notable in our findings was the fact that up until this point, darknet market activity appeared to be impervious to Bitcoin market activity. Fluctuations in Bitcoin’s value, which have always been common, rarely appeared to play a role in darknet market consumers’ purchasing activity. However, when Bitcoin’s price began to fall in mid-March following the first round of U.S. lockdowns, so too did darknet market activity.
 But this change would prove to only be temporary. Starting around May, darknet market revenue returned to its previous state, no longer shifting in sync with Bitcoin’s price. Since then, darknet markets’ monthly revenue has steadily grown, save for small drops in September and November, which largely fall in line with seasonal trends.
  With these latest developments, overall darknet market revenue for 2020 has surpassed that of 2019, with one month left to go. But while total revenue may not change, other numbers indicate that tough times could be ahead for darknet markets.
  The graph above shows both total darknet market revenue by year, as well as the total number of transfers to darknet markets, which we can use to roughly approximate the number of individual customers and purchases. Interestingly, we see that while revenue has already surpassed its 2019 total (again, with one month left to go), total transfers to darknet markets stand at just over 9 million — well below pace to meet the 2019 total of over 12.0 million. The numbers show that customers in 2020 are making fewer purchases but for larger amounts per purchase compared to 2019. This could indicate that casual buyers or those buying drugs for personal use are shifting away from darknet markets, while those buying in larger amounts — either for personal use or to sell to others — are purchasing more. It could also mean that some casual buyers have begun placing larger orders to stock up amidst uncertainty.
 We’ve also seen more darknet market closures in 2020, including prominent markets like Flugsvamp 2.0 and Empire. We see this reflected in the graph below, which shows the number of active markets in each month (active meaning the market has received at least $100 worth of cryptocurrency in a given month) since January 2015.
  While some markets claim their closures are only temporary, the 37 darknet markets active in November 2020 is the lowest total since November 2017. We saw no such decline in 2019. In fact, this year’s decline in active markets follows a period of sustained growth in the number of active markets from 2018 through February 2020.
  It’s often difficult to tell why markets shut down when they do, as administrators often pull exit scams, in which the market ceases operations but publicly appears to still be active so that administrators can continue collecting money from purchases that will never be fulfilled. Other markets have fallen victim to denial-of-service (DoS) attacks from other markets, in some cases closing as an apparent result. We saw both phenomena in the case of Empire Market, a large and widely trusted darknet market whose operators  exit scammed earlier this year two days after being hit by a DoS attack.
  Covid has undoubtedly hindered darknet markets’ sales and operations by causing supply chain disruptions, particularly shipping delays. Darknet market observers have seen this in the form of customer complaints on darknet market-focused forums like Dread and in notes from vendors setting expectations for buyers.
   The evidence isn’t just anecdotal either. Criminology researchers Andréanne Bergeron, David Décary-Hétu, and Luca Giommoni recently  published a study analyzing hundreds of darknet market drug sales made before and after Covid lockdowns began in the U.S. and Europe to determine how much the virus impacted operations. They found that in the pre-Covid period of January 1 to March 21 2020, between 60% and 100% of all orders on any given day were successful. After Covid lockdowns began, however, the study found that just 21% of all deliveries were successful and on time. Customers and vendors blaming Covid for longer delivery times therefore appear to be correct.
 But are shipping delays and other Covid-related operational difficulties causing markets to shut down? We followed up with Lecturer Andréanne Bergeron and Professor David Décary-Hétu, two of the researchers behind the study, to ask their opinion. They reiterated their point that Covid has caused ongoing darknet market delivery delays by placing more strain on postal services. “The world hasn’t gone back to normal yet, so it is unsurprising that the market hasn’t corrected itself yet. Postal services aren’t doing great,” said Bergeron.
 However, the researchers didn’t think that any of the darknet market closures in 2020 were a direct result of Covid. “It’s becoming more challenging than ever to run a darknet market — you have to enable security and guard against DoS attacks, and then on top of that there’s competition. All of these factors limit the availability of drugs,” said Décary-Hétu. He believes that these natural forces of competition, rather than the Covid crisis, were the real reason for increased closures, pointing to Chainalysis data to make his point.
 “Excluding Hydra, if all darknet markets take in $250 million per year and administrators make 5% commission, that’s $12.5 million total divided by all the markets, where a lot of employees have to be paid. It’s simply not worth the risk of spending 100+ years in jail,” said Décary-Hétu.
  Darknet markets appear to be in a precarious position in 2020, with several closing down and the remainder relying on a shrinking pool of customers for revenue. Counterintuitively, and despite its impact on shipping times, Covid doesn’t appear to be the primary cause of these issues. Instead, darknet market consolidation may be the result of competitive forces endemic to the category itself, with Covid at most simply speeding up a trend that already existed.
 We see a similar dynamic play out in so-called  winner-takes-all markets like technology, in which competition over time naturally whittles the market down to the biggest, most efficient players. There are of course key differences between darknet markets and technology companies — Apple, for instance, doesn’t need to worry about being shut down by law enforcement. But still, as Professor Décary-Hétu points out, darknet markets are a tough business, and the dwindling number of markets suggests that not all of those standing today will survive.

title: 到2020年11月，只有37个暗网市场活跃，低于2月份的峰值59，这是因为出口欺诈，DDoS攻击和大流行加速了市场整合
title_en: Only 37 darknet markets were active in Nov. 2020, down from a peak of 59 in Feb., as exit scams, DDoS attacks, and the pandemic accelerate market consolidation
